trends.name: "no no no NO NO NO"

def.normie: A short audio clip where someone says 'no no no' quietly and then yells 'NO NO NO' as panic sets in. People put it over moments where everything is about to go wrong — a surprise meeting invite, an email arriving five minutes before logging off, finding out your friend told 'that' story.
def.normal: The "I Green Screen Things" sound where escalating "no"s match an escalating crash-out. Works as a CapCut template and as a lip-sync — every niche has its own version of the moment they realised everything was about to go wrong.
def.zoomer: Pure pre-crashout audio. The brilliance is the build: quiet refusal, public denial, then the howl. Every flavour has its version now — corporate, parenting, group chat — which means we are exactly one brand activation away from the format being unusable, but right now it absolutely cooks.

trends.origin: I Green Screen Things audio, TikTok 2026

trends.name: "Hate That I Made You Love Me" mashup

def.normie: A new mashup using Ariana Grande's song 'Petal' that creators have been jumping on this week. It's mostly used in seven-second lip-sync clips about a complicated love-hate situation.
def.normal: Mashup audio that started rising after Ariana Grande's Petal dropped. Fast-growing as a lip-sync trend; trend trackers flagged it as the must-use sound of June 8 week.
def.zoomer: Petal release, mashup wave, lip-sync season — textbook. The window is open and the algorithm is generous if you're early; by week three it will be every brand account explaining their wifi password.

trends.vid: hate that i made you love me trend

trends.name: KitKat Heist memes

def.normie: Last month's huge meme about a real-life European theft of around 400,000 KitKat bars. Still funny, but the joke has matured from breaking-news shock to museum-piece status as new things take over the feed.
def.normal: Memes about the real ~400,000 KitKat theft in Europe. Peaked late May/early June; still in heavy rotation but the freshness curve is bending — the documentary jokes have written themselves into a corner.
def.zoomer: Down from full-volume Heat™ but the cinematic universe is canon. The 'who took the bars' detective edits are still arriving; the brand-account stage came and went; now it's drifting into the reference-meme zone where it joins 'I am the storm' as something you reach for in replies.

trends.origin: Real European logistics theft → meme, mid-2026

trends.name: Nihilistic Penguin

def.normie: Old footage of a penguin walking alone toward the mountains instead of the sea (he is, scientifically, lost) has become the official mascot of opting out of everything. Used for burnout posts, quitting jobs, leaving group chats.
def.normal: Werner Herzog's 2007 documentary penguin walking inland to certain doom became 2026's mascot for opting out. Brands like BMW and Lidl have used it; LinkedIn is now in the saturation phase that usually signals end of cycle.
def.zoomer: Herzog filmed him in 2007, we caught up emotionally in 2026, and now he's on a deck somewhere illustrating Q3 burnout metrics. Peak emotional efficiency: zero edits, infinite captions. The LinkedIn arrival is normally the four-minute warning.

trends.origin: 'Encounters at the End of the World' (2007) → meme, 2026

def.normie: A format where dramatic saxophone music starts quietly and gets louder as a situation gets worse. The sax kicks in at full blast right when everything goes wrong — works for any 'this is fine → oh no' moment.
def.normal: The escalation-saxophone format: music swells as the on-screen situation collapses, the sax hits full volume at the moment of chaos. Simple, universal, endlessly remixable — climbed past KitKat this week.
def.zoomer: The sax went full crescendo and overtook the timeline. Now mutating into regional and genre versions — orchestral, drill, polka — which is usually the last stop before saturation. Currently the most reliable laugh you can manufacture in vertical video.

trends.name: I am my perimeter

def.normie: A video-game streamer, ambushed mid-match, calmly said 'I am my perimeter and my perimeter is me.' The deadpan confidence made it a catchphrase for being totally focused and unbothered.
def.normal: From Snapiyya's Rainbow Six Siege stream: ambushed from three sides, he delivers the line with total calm. Now an all-purpose 'locked in' declaration; gym posts have fully colonised it, which usually signals one month to peak fade.
def.zoomer: Bro got rushed and achieved enlightenment on stream. The line works because he genuinely meant it. Gym TikTok has fully claimed it now, which is the universally-recognised four-week countdown to retirement, but those four weeks are good ones.

trends.origin: Snapiyya / Rainbow Six Siege stream clip, 2026

def.normie: 'AI slop' is the term for cheap, mass-produced AI content clogging up feeds. The fight about it — what counts, who is to blame, whether platforms should label it — is everywhere, and intensifying.
def.normal: Backlash against low-effort AI content is dominating commentary feeds. Hyperreal AI videos that look indistinguishable from reality keep escalating the fight; 'clanker' and 'slop' are now standard vocabulary across platforms.
def.zoomer: The Slop Wars: phase two. Now with hyperreal videos passing as news and detection tools that don't really work. The discourse about slop now outproduces the slop, which is itself slop, which is the joke. Nobody is winning except the engagement.

def.normie: X just rolled out a 'Certified Banger' badge for viral posts. Within hours the internet started either celebrating it, mocking it, or fighting about which posts deserved it. A textbook platform-change discourse cycle in under a week.
def.normal: X's new viral-post badge launched the first week of June and immediately became the discourse. Three lanes: posts proudly displaying it, posts dunking on the very concept, and 'why did THIS get one' callouts. The platform-change cycle, speedrun.
def.zoomer: Elon-era platform feature drops as content. By Wednesday people were posting 'no banger badge after that?' in replies as the new dunk format. The badge is the meme; the absence of the badge is the meme; the cope when you don't get one is the meme. Beautiful synergy.

def.normie: A movement of people saying memes have gotten worse — too much AI-made and brain-rot content — and calling for a return to older, simpler human-made internet humour. Now its own genre, complete with 2012 throwback rage comics.
def.normal: The push for nostalgic, authentic memes as reaction to AI slop and brain-rot fatigue. Now an aesthetic in its own right: rage comics, jpeg artefacts, ironic Impact font. Still rising; commentary creators are leaning into it hard.
def.zoomer: The Reset is a meme about wanting better memes, which is correctly very us. Everyone reposting 2012 rage comics 'ironically' and feeling something real. The 'ironic' is doing a lot of work; we just like them. That's allowed.

def.normie: An insult for robots, AI, or people who rely on AI too much — borrowed from Star Wars, where soldiers called battle droids 'clankers'. As AI content has flooded feeds, the word has settled in as everyday slang.
def.normal: Star Wars' battle-droid slur, repurposed as the internet's everyday anti-AI insult. Aimed at bots, AI content farms, and humans outsourcing their personality to a chatbot. Steady high heat, becoming durable vocabulary rather than a flash trend.
def.zoomer: Now in the 'said unironically by your dad in three months' phase. Successor terms ('wireback', 'toaster') are circling but clanker has staying power because it's specific, percussive, and cosplays as Star Wars trivia. Free aura when applied correctly.

def.normie: To completely lose your cool — a meltdown, usually public, often over something small. 'He crashed out over a parking ticket.' One of the most-used slang terms of the past year.
def.normal: Losing emotional control spectacularly; 'a crashout' is the episode or the person prone to them. Now embedded in mainstream vocabulary — outlets and creators use it casually without explanation.
def.zoomer: Officially load-bearing slang. Pundits use it now, which historically kills a word, but this one survives because everyone has a crashout receipt to share. The 'on the verge of a crash out' construction is the durable form.

def.normie: A new way to call someone empty or absent — like the '404 page not found' error. 'He's so 404 coded' = nobody's home upstairs. Was an incoming signal on the radar last week; now arriving.
def.normal: The '-coded' suffix found its tech-meme breakout. 'Page not found' as personality diagnosis, spreading from tech accounts into general slang exactly as our radar predicted last cycle.
def.zoomer: Radar called it, the timeline confirmed it. The '-coded' suffix family keeps printing — error-coded, beta-coded, captcha-coded all incubating. 404 is the breakout because everyone has seen the error and recognises the diagnosis on sight.

def.normie: A huge fan theory claiming the Stranger Things finale wasn't real — that the ending was an illusion and a secret final episode exists. Creators keep denying it; the theory keeps growing anyway, though it's cooled this week.
def.normal: The viral theory that the Stranger Things finale was an in-universe illusion concealing a hidden episode. Equal parts close-reading, denial-as-coping, and engagement farming. Past its discourse peak but still gathering new posts.
def.zoomer: Speedrun denial fandom %, complete. Now in the long-tail phase where every dropped frame is 'proof'. The theory will outlive the show's relevance, which is the point of fan theories now.

def.normie: A mash-up of 'chopped' (unattractive) and 'doppelganger': an unflattering lookalike. The celebrity side-by-side format peaked fast and is already cooling.
def.normal: 'Chopped' + 'doppelganger' = blunt comparison format. The celebrity side-by-side strain peaked late May; now in the predictable post-saturation slide as the cruelty becomes formulaic.
def.zoomer: The format peaked the second someone got choppelganger'd with their own filtered photo. Burning out exactly on schedule for insult formats — about one school year. Word will survive longer than the format that birthed it.

def.normie: The joke that consuming too much silly internet content rots your brain — Oxford's 2024 word of the year. Still universally understood, but no longer a fresh way to describe anything.
def.normal: The supposed mental decay from absurd internet content. After two dominant years, now the named enemy of the Great Meme Reset — universally understood, no longer cutting edge. Slow steady decline this month.
def.zoomer: The old guard. Oxford crowned it, your aunt says it, and the Reset crowd is openly hostile. Saying you have brain rot is no longer a flex — it's a census statistic. The vocabulary will outlive the era by years.

battles.q: Which audio is winning this week?
Which X feature became its own meme?

hot: the official pre-crashout soundtrack of June
escalation has a brass section now
an X feature speedran being its own meme
died: deported by the Great Meme Reset
the irony collapsed in on itself
faded before the algorithm could find it
incoming: the format mocking people upset their post didn't get the badge is forming — expect peak by Friday
tip: This sound is at peak — use it now, not next week. The window for these audio trends is 10-14 days; jump on it with a panic-build that pays off in chaos. CapCut templates already exist.
weird: Our radar flagged this last cycle as 'incoming'. It crossed into general slang within two weeks. The radar works, occasionally, when the moon is in Aquarius.

radar.name: "Banger badge cope"
radar.sig: This format is about to explode
radar.det: Posts mocking people whose viral tweets *didn't* get X's new Certified Banger badge are forming a template. The cope replies are the content.

radar.name: Petal album residue
radar.det: Beyond the 'hate that I made you love me' mashup, two more cuts from Ariana Grande's Petal are getting CapCut-templated. Album-residue waves can produce 3-4 spin-off audios over a month.

radar.name: PlayStation actual gameplay
radar.sig: Gaming circles are processing a vibe shift
radar.det: Sony's June State of Play broke a multi-year pattern of CGI-only reveals, and gaming Twitter is treating it as a small cultural event. Reaction edits incoming.

radar.name: Heist-core aesthetic
radar.sig: Niche but spreading across food and fashion
radar.det: Post-KitKat, playful 'heist' aesthetics — ski masks and snack hauls, 'stealing' recipes, heist-movie grading on hauls — are bubbling in food and fashion content. Slower burn than its parent meme.

radar.name: Clanker court
radar.sig: AI-on-trial skits are becoming a genre
radar.det: Mock-trial videos putting AI tools and bot accounts on trial are building in commentary and gaming circles — the comedic arm of slop discourse. Multiple creators independently arriving at the same format is the signal.

radar.name: Analog posting
radar.sig: The Reset's aesthetic wing is gaining ground
radar.det: Film photos, unedited clips, typed-and-photographed text posts — deliberately low-tech content as anti-slop signalling. Adjacent to the Great Meme Reset and growing alongside it.

radar.name: "-coded" suffix wave
radar.det: After 404 coded broke through, error-coded, beta-coded, captcha-coded and CAPTCHA-coded are incubating. The pattern produces new entries roughly weekly.

radar.name: Heatwave content
radar.sig: Weather is about to drive a content wave
radar.det: European heatwave coverage is starting to spawn a familiar summer-discourse cycle: AC takes, ice-cream tier lists, 'they don't believe in AC' jokes. Format-ready by next week.
